svg.text: CSL — CHINESE SIGN LANGUAGE
NOTATION — EAST ASIAN SIGN LANGUAGES
CSL: CHINESE SIGN LANGUAGE
CSL: most widely used sign language in the world by population (~20M users)
Developed primarily in Shanghai (1887: first school for Deaf in China)
Two major varieties: Shanghai SL and Beijing SL (partially unified post-1949)
CSL MANUAL SYSTEMS
Chinese finger-spelling: handshapes for Mandarin pinyin sounds (zhuyin-based)
CSL also uses some signs derived from written Chinese character shapes
Iconic signs: many CSL signs visually trace Chinese characters (unique feature)
No established standard writing system for CSL; video-primary
CSL RECOGNITION
CSL: recognized in China's Disability Act; taught in Deaf schools
CODA (China Organization of Deaf Affairs): promotes CSL standardization
TAIWAN SIGN LANGUAGE (TSL) NOTE
TSL: used in Taiwan; different from CSL (separate development under Japan)
TSL is closer to JSL historically; CSL is mainland Chinese
KSL · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
KOREAN SIGN LANGUAGE (KSL)
KSL: ~300,000 users in South Korea; Seoul-centered
First school: Pyongyang (1909, Japanese colonial period); JSL influence
KSL developed from JSL (Japanese SL) base during colonial period (1910-1945)
KSL FINGERSPELLING
KSL: one-handed manual alphabet for hangul syllables
Handshapes represent Korean syllable blocks (not individual letters)
Very different from Western fingerspelling systems
NOTATION COMPARISON
CSL: fingerspelling based on pinyin romanization
KSL: fingerspelling based on hangul syllable-block system
Both: no standard written form; video is primary medium
Both: character-based scripts inform sign language notation approaches
PLATE 65a ALT · CSL / KSL · NOTATION
